name: November Mine
short_description: Historic gold prospect in the Black Hills region. Search for quartz veins and feldspar in this classic South Dakota mining area at 6,000+ ft elevation.
description:
November Mine is a historic prospect location in Custer County's gold-bearing Black Hills region. The site sits at 6,009 feet elevation on gentle slopes typical of the high plateau terrain found throughout this mineral-rich area. This location has historical significance as part of South Dakota's gold mining heritage, when prospectors actively worked claims throughout the Black Hills during the late 1800s and early 1900s.

The geology here reflects the complex metamorphic and igneous formations that characterize the Black Hills uplift. Quartz veins cut through feldspar-rich host rock, creating natural pathways where gold particles concentrate. The gentle slopes make this an accessible location for rockhounders interested in understanding gold mineralization patterns and seeing period mining workings.

Visitors should expect to find quartz specimens, feldspar fragments, and scattered mica flakes throughout the area. The site offers insight into how historic miners identified and worked promising claims. Surface collecting yields small quartz pieces and feldspar samples. More experienced rockhounders can examine vein structures and formation patterns.

This location works well for educational rockhounding and those interested in mining history. The moderate elevation and gentle terrain make it suitable for a wide range of visitors. The Black Hills region contains numerous similar prospects, making this an excellent base location for exploring the broader mineralization patterns of western South Dakota.
history: The November Mine sits within the Black Hills uplift, a Precambrian metamorphic dome where Late Cretaceous and Tertiary hydrothermal fluids deposited gold-bearing quartz veins along fracture zones. This area was actively prospected during South Dakota's gold rush era, when miners worked hundreds of small claims throughout the Black Hills searching for economic gold concentrations.
tips: Search along the main quartz veins and their weathered outcrops on the hillsides - look for white quartz fragments and feldspar-rich material in the surrounding matrix. Bring a hand lens to spot small gold flakes in quartz samples, and carry a small prospector's pick or rock hammer to carefully extract specimens without damaging the vein structures. Look for iron staining and rust-colored zones around quartz outcrops, as these indicate areas where original mineralization has oxidized, often concentrating gold particles in surface materials.
terrain_notes: Elevation 6,009 feet with gentle slopes and mixed grass and scattered timber vegetation. Terrain is typical high-elevation Black Hills plateau with stable footing and minimal loose rock hazards. Walking from parking area to productive digging areas is generally short (under 1 mile) with steady but manageable grades. Rocky outcrops are stable and accessible.
best_season: June - September. Higher elevations in the Black Hills experience significant snow and cold from October through May, making access difficult. Summer months provide reliable weather, manageable road conditions, and safe hiking.
written_directions: From Custer, take US 385 north for approximately 8 miles toward Hill City. Watch for old mining area access roads or mining claim markers. The November Mine is located on gentle slopes near established mining prospects in this section of the Black Hills. Local BLM office in Custer can provide specific access directions and confirm current road conditions. Standard passenger vehicles can typically reach parking areas on US 385 during summer months.
road_conditions: US 385 is paved. Access roads to the mining area may be unpaved and rough; inquire with local BLM office before visiting.
parking_notes: Pullouts along US 385 near the mine location. Limited formal parking; space for 2-4 vehicles typical of BLM mining area access points.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Respect historic mining structures and claim markers. All collection must be from surface materials; do not enter or tunnel into old mine workings.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Do not enter abandoned mine shafts or tunnels
Respect all claim markers and historic structures
nearest_services: Custer, SD - approximately 8 miles south. Full services including gas, restaurants, lodging, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers. Unpaved access roads to mining areas; standard hiking boots recommended. No wheelchair access to the mine itself. Elevation of 6,000+ feet may affect visitors with respiratory sensitivity.

faq.question: What gemstones and minerals can I find at November Mine in South Dakota?
faq.answer: November Mine is primarily known for gold, quartz, and feldspar specimens. Surface rockhounders commonly find white quartz fragments, feldspar pieces, and scattered mica flakes. Small gold particles may be visible in quartz samples when examined with a hand lens, offering insight into the historical gold mineralization of the Black Hills region.

faq.question: Is a permit required to rockhound at November Mine near Custer?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at November Mine. As a public BLM location, you can collect up to 25 lbs per day under Forest Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA) regulations. However, you must respect all claim markers and historic mining structures on the site.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to visit November Mine for rockhounding?
faq.answer: June through September is optimal for visiting November Mine. The Black Hills at 6,009 feet elevation experiences significant snow and cold weather October through May, making roads less accessible and hiking conditions hazardous. Summer months provide stable weather and clear road access.

faq.question: How far is November Mine from Custer, and what are the driving directions?
faq.answer: November Mine is approximately 8 miles north of Custer via US 385. From downtown Custer, take US 385 north toward Hill City and watch for mining area access roads and claim markers. Contact the local BLM office in Custer for specific current road conditions and parking details.

faq.question: Is November Mine rockhounding site suitable for families and beginning rockhounders?
faq.answer: Yes, November Mine is beginner-friendly and suitable for families with children ages 6 and up. The gentle slopes and easy terrain make it accessible to most fitness levels. Surface collecting requires no special equipment, though a hand lens and small prospector's pick enhance the experience. The site's historical significance also provides educational value for learning about Black Hills mining.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need to collect at November Mine?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots suitable for rocky terrain, a hand lens to spot gold particles in quartz samples, and a small prospector's pick or rock hammer for safely extracting specimens. A collection bag and water are essential. Since cell service is spotty, inform someone of your location and expected return time. Standard passenger vehicles can access parking areas, though high-clearance vehicles are safer for rough access roads.

meta_description: Hunt for gold, quartz, and feldspar at November Mine near Custer. Public BLM access, beginner-friendly, June-September. Historic Black Hills prospect.
